narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your journey toward law school, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to passing the bar exam as a bit too uncertain—tipping the scales at a 53% chance of not making it. Picture your story as the model did: it started with your strong first-year decile of 2 at school, but by your third year, staying in that same 2nd decile became the biggest signal pulling toward challenges ahead, suggesting the coursework didn't build the momentum we typically see in bar passers. Your LSAT score of 33 added to that caution, as it's on the lower side of what helps students power through the exam's demands. Early on, your undergraduate GPA of 2.2 echoed those concerns, hinting at a foundation that might need extra shoring up. Layered in was your low family income, which the model associates with added hurdles like fewer resources for prep courses or study time—something we've seen trip up others. Your background as a Black male full-time student wove in further, reflecting patterns where systemic barriers and rigorous schedules make the bar a steeper climb. Even small details, like going full-time without part-time flexibility, nudged the outlook downward. Together, these pieces painted a picture of resilience tested by consistent academic deciles, modest entry scores, and real-life pressures that have historically made bar success elusive for profiles like yours. It's not a judgment on your potential—far from it—but a data-driven glimpse showing where the risks stack up highest. If you boost that third-year decile or LSAT through retakes or targeted prep, your story could shift dramatically next time. You've got the drive; now it's about aligning the numbers that open doors. Hang in there—we're rooting for your comeback.
